author: John Chrysostom
book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.

classification.cautionReason: No major mainstream heresy warning is attached by the preview classifier.

lineage: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.

ccel.title: NPNF1-10. St. Chrysostom: Homilies on the Gospel of Saint Matthew

source.title: NPNF1-10. St. Chrysostom: Homilies on the Gospel of Saint Matthew

title: Matthew XXVII. 27-29.

source.title: NPNF1-10. St. Chrysostom: Homilies on the Gospel of Saint Matthew

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “Then the soldiers of the governor took Jesus into the common hall,and gathered unto him the whole band of soldiers; and they stripped Him, and put on Him a purple robe; and when they had platted a crown of thorns, they put it on His head, and a reed in His right hand; and they bowed the knee before Him, and mocked Him, saying, Hail, king of the Jews.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: As though on some signal the devil then was entering in triumphinto all.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For, be it that Jews pining with envy and jealousy were mad against Him, as to the soldiers, whence was it, and from what sort of cause?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Is it not clear that it was the devil who was then entering in fury into the hearts of all?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For indeed they made a pleasure of their insults against Him, being a savage and ruthless set.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: I mean that, when they ought to have been awestruck, when they ought to have wept, which even the people did, this they did not, but, on the contrary, were despiteful, and insolent; perhaps themselves also seeking to please the Jews, or it may be doing all in conformity to their own evil nature.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And the insults were different, and varied.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For that Divine Head at one time they buffeted, at another they insulted with the crown of thorns, at another they smote with the reed, men unholy and accursed!

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: What plea shall we have after this for being moved by injuries, after Christ suffered these things?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For what was done was the utmost limit of insolence.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For not one member, but the whole entire body throughout was made an object of insolence; the head through the crown, and the reed, and the buffeting; the face, being spit upon; the cheeks, being smitten with the palms of the hands; the whole body by the stripes, by being wrapped in the robe, and by the pretended worship; the hand by the reed, which they gave him to hold instead of a sceptre; the mouth again by the offering of the vinegar.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: What could be more grievous than these things?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: What more insulting?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For the things that were done go beyond all language.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For as though they were afraid lest they should seem to fall short at all in the crime, having killed the prophets with their own hands, but this man with the sentence of a judge, so they do in every deed; and make it the work of their own hands, and condemn and sentence both among themselves and before Pilate, saying, “His blood be on us and on our children,”and insult Him, and do despite unto Him themselves, binding Him, leading Him away, and render themselves authors of the spiteful acts done by the soldiers, and nail Him to the cross, and revile Him, and spit at Him, and deride Him.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For Pilate contributed nothing in this matter, but they themselves did every thing, becoming accusers, and judges, and executioners, and all.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And these things are read amongst us, when all meet together.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For that the heathens may not say, that ye display to people and nations the things that are glorious and illustrious, such as the signs and the miracles, but that ye hide these which are matters of reproach; the grace of the Spirit hath brought it to pass, that in the full festival, when men in multitude and women are present, and all, as one may say, at the great eve of the passover, then all these things should be read; when the whole world is present, then are all these acts proclaimed with a clear voice.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And these being read, and made known to all, Christ is believed to be God and, besides all the rest, is worshipped, even because of this, that He vouchsafed to stoop so much for us as actually to suffer these things, and to teach us all virtue.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: These things then let us read continually; for indeed great is the gain, great the advantage to be thence obtained.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For when thou seest Him, both by gestures and by deeds, mocked and worshipped with so much derision, and beaten and suffering the utmost insults, though thou be very stone, thou wilt become softer than any wax, and wilt cast out of thy soul all haughtiness.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Hear therefore also what follows.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For after “they had mocked Him, they led Him to crucify Him,”it is said, and when they had stripped Him, they took His garments, and sat down and watched Him, when He should die.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And they divide His garments amongst them, which sort of thing is done in the case of very vile and abject criminals, and such as have no one belonging to them, and are in utter desolation.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: They parted the garments, by which such great miracles were done.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But they wrought none now, Christ restraining His unspeakable power.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And this was no small addition of insult.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For as to one base and abject, as I said, and the vilest of all men; so do they dare to do all things.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: To the thieves at any rate they did nothing of the kind, but to Christ they dare it all.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And they crucified Him in the midst of them, that He might share in their reputation.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And they gave Him gall to drink, and this to insult Him, but He would not.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But another saith, that having tasted it, He said, “It is finished.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And what meaneth, “It is finished?”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: The prophecy was fulfilled concerning Him.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “For they gave me,” it is said, “gall for my meat, and for my thirst they gave me vinegar to drink.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But neither doth that evangelist indicate that He drank, for merely to taste differs not from not drinking, but hath one and the same signification.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But nevertheless not even here doth their contumely stop, but after having stripped and crucified Him, and offered Him vinegar, they proceeded still further, and beholding Him impaled upon the cross, they revile Him, both they themselves and the passers by; and this was more grievous than all, that on the charge of being an impostor and deceiver He suffered these things, and as a boaster, and vainly pretending what He said.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Therefore they both crucified Him publicly, that they might make a show of it in the sight of all; and therefore also they did it by the hands of the soldiers, that these things being perpetrated even by a public tribunal, the insult might be the greater.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And yet who would not have been moved by the multitude that was following Him, and lamenting Him?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Nay, not these wild beasts.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Wherefore also He to the multitude vouchsafes an answer, but to these men not so.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For after having done what they would, they endeavor also to injure His honor, fearing His resurrection.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Therefore they say these things publicly, and crucified thieves with Him, and wishing to prove Him a deceiver, they say, “Thou that destroyest the temple, and buildest it in three days come down from the cross.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For since on telling Pilate to remove the accusation (this was the writing, “The king of the Jews”), they prevailed not, but he persevered in saying, “What I have written, I have written,”they then endeavor by their derision of Him to show that He is not a king.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Wherefore they said those things, and also these.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: If “He is the king of Israel, let Him come down now from the cross.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: He saved others, Himself He cannot save,”aiming hereby to bring discredit even on His former miracles.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And again, “If He be Son of God, and He will have Him, let Him save Him.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: O execrable; most execrable!

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: What, were not the prophets prophets, nor the righteous men righteous, because God rescued them not out of their dangers.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Nay surely they were, though suffering these things.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: What then could be equal to your folly?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For if the coming of the dangers upon them did not injure their honor with you, how much more in the case of this man, was it wrong for you to be offended, when both by what He did, by what He said, He was ever correcting beforehand this suspicion of yours.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Yet nevertheless, even when these things were said and done, they prevailed nothing, not even at the very time.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: At any rate, he, who was depraved in such great wickedness, and who had spent his whole life in murders and house-breakings, when these things were being said, then confessed Him, and made mention of a kingdom, and the people bewailed Him.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And yet the things that were done seemed to testify the contrary in the eyes of those who knew not the mysterious dispensations, that He was weak and of no power, nevertheless truth prevailed even by the contrary things.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Hearing then these things, let us arm ourselves against all rage, against all anger.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Shouldest thou perceive thy heart swelling, seal thy breast setting upon it the cross.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Call to mind some one of the things that then took place, and thou wilt cast out as dust all rage by the recollection of the things that were done.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Consider the words, the actions; consider that He is Lord, and thou servant.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: He is suffering for thee, thou for thyself; He in behalf of them who had been benefited by Him and had crucified Him, thou in behalf of thyself; He in behalf of them who had used Him despitefully, thou oftentimes at the hands of them who have been injured.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: He in the sight of the whole city, or rather of the whole people of the Jews, both strangers, and those of the country, before whom He spake those merciful words, but thou in the presence of few; and what was more insulting to Him, that even His disciples forsook Him.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For those, who before paid Him attention, had deserted Him, but His enemies and foes, having got Him in the midst of themselves on the cross, insulted, reviled, mocked, derided, scoffed at Him, Jews and soldiers from below, from above thieves on either side: for indeed the thieves insulted, and upbraided Him both of them.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: How then saith Luke that one “rebuked?”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Both things were done, for at first both upbraided Him, but afterwards one did so no more.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For that thou mightest not think the thing had been done by any agreement, or that the thief was not a thief, by his insolence he showeth thee, that up on the cross he was a thief and an enemy, and at once was changed.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Considering then all these things, control thyself.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For what sufferest thou like what thy Lord suffered?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Wast thou publicly insulted?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But not like these things.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Art thou mocked? yet not thy whole body, not being thus scourged, and stripped.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And even if thou wast buffeted, yet not like this.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And add to this, I pray thee, by whom, and wherefore, and when, and who it was; and (the most grievous matter) that these things being done, no one found fault, no one blamed what was done, but on the contrary all rather approved, and joined in mocking Him and in jeering at Him; and as a boaster, impostor, and deceiver, and not able to prove in His works the things that He said, so did they revile Him.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But He held His peace to all, preparing for us the most powerful incentives to long suffering.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But we, though hearing such things, are not patient so much as to servants, but we rush and kick worse than wild asses, with respect to injuries against ourselves, being savage and inhuman; but of those against God not making much account.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And with respect to friends too we have the same disposition; should any one vex us, we bear it not; should he insult us, we are savage more than wild beasts, we who are reading these things every day.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: A disciple betrayed Him, the rest forsook Him and fled, they that had been benefited by Him spat at Him, the servants of the high priest smote Him with the palm of the hand, the soldiers buffeted Him; they that passed by jeered Him and reviled Him, the thieves accused Him; and to no man did He utter a word, but by silence overcame all; instructing thee by His actions, that the more meekly thou shalt endure, the more wilt thou prevail over them that do thee evil, and wilt be an object of admiration before all.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For who will not admire him that endures with forbearance the insults he receives from them that are using him despitefully?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For even as, though any man suffer justly, yet enduring the evil meekly, he is considered by the more part to suffer unjustly; so though one suffer unjustly, yet if he be violent, he will get the suspicion of suffering justly, and will be an object of ridicule, as being dragged captive by his anger, and losing his own nobility.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For such a one, we must not call so much as a freeman, though he be lord over ten thousand servants.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But did some person exceedingly provoke thee?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And what of that?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For then should self-control be shown, since when there is no one to vex, we see even the wild beasts gentle; for neither are they always savage, but when any one rouses them.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And we therefore, if we are only then quiet, when there is no one provoking us, what advantage have we over them.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For they are both oftentimes justly indignant, and have much excuse, for by being stirred and goaded are they roused, and besides these things they are devoid of reason, and have savageness in their nature.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But whence, I pray thee, canst thou find a plea for being savage and fierce?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: What hardship hast thou suffered?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Hast thou been robbed?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For this self-same reason shouldest thou endure it, so as to gain more amply.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But wast thou deprived of character?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And what is this?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Thy condition is in no way worsened by this, if thou practise self-command.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But if thou sufferest no grievance, whence art thou angry with him that hath done thee no harm, but hath even benefited thee?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For they who honor, make them that are not watchful the more vain; but they who insult and despise render those that take heed to themselves more steadfast.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For the careless are more injured by being honored than by being insulted.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And the one set of persons, if we be sober, become to us authors of self-control, but the others excite our pride, they fill us with boastfulness, vainglory, folly, they make our soul the feebler.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And to this fathers bear witness, who do not flatter their own children so much as they chide them, fearing lest from the praise they should receive any harm, and their teachers use the same remedy to them.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: So that if we are to avoid any one, it should be those that flatter us rather than those that insult us; for this bait brings greater mischief than insult to them, who do not take heed, and it is more difficult to control this feeling than that.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And the reward too is far more abundant from thence, and the admiration greater.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For indeed it is more worthy of admiration to see a man insulted, and not moved, than beaten and smitten, and not falling.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And how is it possible not to be moved? one may say.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Hath any one insulted thee?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Place the sign upon thy breast, call to mind all the things that were then done; and all is quenched.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Consider not the insults only, but if also any good hath been ever done unto thee, by him that hath insulted thee, and straightway thou wilt become meek, or rather consider before all things the fear of God, and soon thou wilt be mild and gentle.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Together with these things even from thine own servants take a lesson concerning these matters; and when thou seest thyself insulting, but thy servant holding his peace, consider that it is possible to practise self-control, and condemn thyself for being violent; and in the very time of offering insults learn not to insult; and thus not even when insulted, wilt thou be vexed.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Consider that he who is insolent is beside himself and mad, and thou wilt not feel indignant, when insulted, since the possessed strike us, and we, so far from being provoked, do rather pity them.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: This do thou also; pity him that is insolent to thee, for he is held in subjection by a dreadful monster, rage, by a grievous demon, anger.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Set him free as he is wrought upon by a grievous demon, and going quickly to ruin.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For so great is this disease as not to need even time for the destruction of him that is seized with it.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Wherefore also one said, “The sway of his fury shall be his fall;”by this most of all showing its tyranny, that in a short time it works great ills, and needs not to continue long with us, so that if in addition to its strength it were apt to last, it would indeed be hard to strive against.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: I should like to show what the man is who insulteth, what he that practises self-control, and to bring nakedly before you the soul of the one and the other.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For thou shouldest see the one like a sea tost with a tempest, but the other like a harbor free from disturbance.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For it is not disturbed by these evil blasts, but puts them to rest easily.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For indeed they who are insulting, do everything in order to make it sting.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: When then they fail of that hope, even they are thenceforth at peace, and go away amended.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For it is impossible that a man, who is angry, should not utterly condemn himself, even as on the other hand it is impossible for one who is not angry to be self-condemned.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For though it be necessary to retaliate, it is possible to do this without anger (and it were more easy and more wise than with anger) and to have no painful feeling.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For if we be willing, the good things will be from ourselves, and we shall be with the grace of God sufficient for our own safety and honor.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For why seekest thou the glory that cometh from another?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Do thou honor thyself, and no one will be able to insult thee; but if thou dishonor thyself, though all should honor thee, thou wilt not be honored.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For like as, unless we put ourselves in an evil state, no one else puts us in such a state; even so unless we insult ourselves, no one else can put us to shame.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For let any man be great and worthy of admiration, and let all men call him an adulterer, a thief, a violater of tombs, a murderer, a robber, and let him be neither provoked or indignant, nor be conscious to himself of any of these crimes, what disgrace will he thence undergo?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: What then, you may say, if many have such an opinion of him?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Not even so is he disgraced, but they bring shame upon themselves, by accounting one, who is not such, to be such.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For tell me, if any one think the sun to be dark, doth he bring an ill name on that heavenly body, or on himself?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Surely on himself, getting himself the character of being blind or mad.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: So also they that account wicked men good, and they that make the opposite error, disgrace themselves.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Wherefore we ought to give the greater diligence, to keep our conscience clear, and to give no handle against ourselves, nor matter for evil suspicion; but if others will be mad, even when this is our disposition, not to care very much, nor to grieve.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For he that hath got the character of a wicked man, being a good man, is in no degree thereby hurt as regards his being such as he is; but he that hath been suspecting another vainly and causelessly, receives the utmost harm; as, on the other hand, the wicked man, if he be supposed to be the contrary, will gain nothing thence, but will both have a heavier judgment, and be led into greater carelessness.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For he that is such and is suspected thereof, may perhaps be humbled, and acknowledge his sins; but when he escapes detection, he falls into a state past feeling.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For if, while all are accusing them, offenders are hardly stirred up to compunction, when so far from accusing them, some even praise them, at what time will they who are living in vice be able to open their eyes?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Hearest thou that Paul also blames for this, that the Corinthians (so far from permitting him that had been guilty of fornication, to acknowledge his own sin), applauding and honoring him, did on the contrary urge him on in vice thereby?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Wherefore, I pray, let us leave the suspicions of the multitude, their insults and their honors, and let us be diligent about one thing only, that we be conscious to ourselves of no evil thing, nor insult our own selves.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For so both here, and in the world to come, we shall enjoy much glory, unto which God grant we all may attain, by the grace and love towards man of our Lord Jesus Christ, to whom be glory world without end.
